verses.text: Sanballat repeatedly invites Nehemiah to meet in the plain of Ono, meaning to harm him, but four times he answers, 'I am doing a great work, so that I cannot come down.' He will not be lured from his task. Distraction is a favourite trap; the focused servant declines even pleasant detours from God's work.

Sanballat repeatedly invites Nehemiah to meet in the plain of Ono, meaning to harm him, but four times he answers, 'I am doing a great work, so that I cannot come down.' He will not be lured from his task. Distraction is a favourite trap; the focused servant declines even pleasant detours from God's work.

He will not be lured from his task. Distraction is a favourite trap; the focused servant declines even pleasant detours from God's work.

Sanballat repeatedly invites Nehemiah to meet in the plain of Ono, meaning to harm him, but four times he answers, 'I am doing a great work, so that I cannot come down.' He will not be lured from his task. Distraction is a favourite trap; the focused servant declines even pleasant detours from God's work.

Then comes an open letter falsely charging Nehemiah with plotting rebellion and seeking to be king. Slander is published to intimidate. The enemy who cannot stop the work by force will try to stain the worker's name.

Then comes an open letter falsely charging Nehemiah with plotting rebellion and seeking to be king. Slander is published to intimidate. The enemy who cannot stop the work by force will try to stain the worker's name.

Then comes an open letter falsely charging Nehemiah with plotting rebellion and seeking to be king. Slander is published to intimidate. The enemy who cannot stop the work by force will try to stain the worker's name.

Nehemiah flatly denies it — 'thou feignest them out of thine own heart' — and prays, 'now therefore, O God, strengthen my hands.' He answers the lie and turns to God. When falsely accused, the believer states the truth plainly and asks God for fresh strength.

Nehemiah flatly denies it — 'thou feignest them out of thine own heart' — and prays, 'now therefore, O God, strengthen my hands.' He answers the lie and turns to God. When falsely accused, the believer states the truth plainly and asks God for fresh strength.

A hired prophet urges him to hide in the temple from supposed assassins, but Nehemiah refuses: 'should such a man as I flee?' discerning the plot to make him sin and be discredited. Courage and discernment together foil the snare. The godly will not save their lives by an act that dishonours God.

A hired prophet urges him to hide in the temple from supposed assassins, but Nehemiah refuses: 'should such a man as I flee?' discerning the plot to make him sin and be discredited. Courage and discernment together foil the snare. The godly will not save their lives by an act that dishonours God.

'My God, think thou upon Tobiah and Sanballat according to these their works.' Nehemiah leaves the reckoning to God. The servant of the Lord need not avenge himself, for the righteous Judge remembers every deed.

The wall is finished in just fifty-two days, and even the enemies are cast down, perceiving the work was wrought of God. The swift success silences the scoffers. When God prospers His people, the very foes are made to confess His hand.

The wall is finished in just fifty-two days, and even the enemies are cast down, perceiving the work was wrought of God. The swift success silences the scoffers. When God prospers His people, the very foes are made to confess His hand.

Yet many nobles of Judah are bound to Tobiah by oath and letters, praising him to Nehemiah and carrying Nehemiah's words back, while Tobiah sends letters to frighten him. The enemy works through false friends within. The faithful must be watchful, for not every threat comes from outside the camp.
